profile.behavior: Active foliage gleaner; rapidly hops and flits through branches, taking insects and spiders from leaves and twig tips.
field_marks: Tiny, compact warbler with a short tail and thin, pointed bill.
Blue-gray head and upperparts with a yellow-green patch centered on the back.
Two crisp white wingbars on dark wings.
Bright yellow throat and upper breast contrasting with a whitish belly.
Pale white eye crescents are conspicuous.
Adult male typically shows a dark and chestnut breast band; females and immature birds are paler and usually lack it.
profile.habitat: Mature, humid deciduous or coniferous forest, especially along streams, swamps, and other wet lowlands with hanging mosses or lichens; also uses wooded and scrubby habitats outside the breeding season.

sources.title: Northern Parula Life History | All About Birds

findings: Identity is supported: the plate shows Northern Parula’s blue-gray upperparts, yellow-green back patch, paired white wingbars, yellow throat/breast, white eye crescents, pale belly, and male chestnut breast marking.

Measurements, scientific name, family, habitat, and behavior text agree with the live Audubon and Cornell Lab species pages. Cornell gives weight as 5–11 g while Audubon gives 6–11 g; the plate’s 6–11 g follows Audubon.

Minor illustration limitations: the male’s dark breast band is understated relative to field-reference photos, and the body/tail read slightly fuller and longer than typical. These are not material identification errors.
The visible profile is anatomically coherent: one head, one beak, two legs, one tail, and a normal paired-wing body plan; no extra or missing body parts are depicted.
No place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation detail appears.

verification_sources.title: Northern Parula Identification | All About Birds
